openQuestions.text: Q1: How does raw verbatim storage vs fact extraction affect memory utility?
Q2: What does the palace taxonomy provide beyond flat tagging?
Q3: How does wake-up layering generate context across L0-L3?
Q4: How do stop and pre-compact hooks shape write timing?
Q5: What is the ChromaDB drawer store doing that our system is not?
Q6: How does the SQLite temporal knowledge graph complement ChromaDB?
Q7: What are the three ingestion modes (project, conversation, heuristic)?
Q8: How does palace graph traversal via shared room metadata work?
Q9: Can we adopt selective raw-evidence handling without the palace mythology?
Q10: Which hook patterns translate to our agent ecosystem?

**What it does**: MemPalace blocks every 15 user turns and always blocks before compaction, then tells the AI to save; optional auto-ingest is backgrounded in the stop hook and synchronous only if `MEMPAL_DIR` is configured. The write path itself is weakly protected: `mempalace_add_drawer` uses deterministic IDs, but it does not actually call the semantic duplicate checker before writing.

**Why it matters for us**: If we copy this pattern into Public, the biggest failure mode is a false sense of safety: the runtime is interrupted, but preservation quality still depends on the model following instructions correctly. That can yield duplicated memories, noisy categorization, partial saves, or blocked UX with no durable improvement. Public’s current compaction transport and JSON-primary `generate-context.js` path are much more deterministic.

**What it does**: Earlier passes rejected raw-verbatim-by-default and a standalone wake-up authority, but they left open the exact form of a bounded read model: whether Public should render a wake-up profile, quote-carrying evidence snippets, or both, and what token cap and retrieval budget those views should obey.

**What it does**: Prior passes converged on a fail-open, JSON-primary checkpoint at compaction time, but they did not settle the exact producer behavior: when it runs, how it dedups, what happens without a spec folder, and how success is measured before compaction continues.

**What it does**: The architecture choice is clear: extend existing bootstrap/status surfaces rather than create a new authority. The unresolved part is the exact hint block: which surface owns it, how long it may be, whether save protocol belongs in it, and how to avoid stale or contradictory advice.

**What it does**: The brief requires a Level 3 packet with checklist and closeout artifacts, but late iterations repeatedly record that the live packet still behaves like a Level 1 packet and lacks the expected reducer and closeout files.

**What it does**: The open question is no longer whether audit and bounded repair are useful; it is what evidence can be logged safely, what must stay metadata-only or redacted, how stderr/handshake cleanliness is preserved, and where `memory_health` remains the sole repair authority.

**What it does**: The remaining uncertainty is not whether a temporal sidecar is architecturally compatible; it is what facts belong there, how invalidation is triggered, how timeline queries surface without competing with the main memory index, and what maintenance/repair lifecycle it needs.

**What it does**: The research now says what to build, but not the measurable thresholds needed to ship it safely: recall floors, acceptable ablation deltas, token caps, p95 budgets, storage-growth ceilings, compaction-survival assertions, and PR-versus-nightly ownership.
**Why it matters**: This determines whether observability increases trust or creates a new poisoning/privacy surface. It is also tightly coupled to how repair flows are documented and gated.

**Why it matters**: This is now less an architecture decision than a prompt-economy and authority-boundary problem. A bad payload can bloat startup, duplicate guidance, or teach the wrong action ordering.
**Why it matters**: This is the highest-leverage adoption, yet it is still missing the contract details that prevent latency spikes, duplicate saves, or false confidence.

**Why it matters**: This is the main unresolved UX lever for startup continuity after compaction or resume. It affects prompt budgets, duplication risk, and whether a new view actually improves recovery over current bootstrap surfaces.
**Why it matters**: This is the only major surviving NEW FEATURE candidate. If its authority boundary is sloppy, it becomes a second truth surface instead of a narrow temporal utility.
**Why it matters**: Until the packet shape is repaired, the research lineage stays harder to validate, synthesize, and hand off. This is now a concrete operational gap, not just documentation drift.
**Why it matters**: Without numeric budgets and CI placement, the roadmap remains a recommendation set rather than an implementable delivery contract.

**Source**: phase-final recommendation blocks rejecting lexical-only search, markdown-first memory, parallel search paths, wrapper-as-backend, or taxonomy-first recall [SOURCE: /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/001-engram-main/research/research.md:179345-179377; /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/002-mex-main/research/research.md:268736-268755; /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/003-modus-memory-main/research/research.md:90134-90173; /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/004-opencode-mnemosyne-main/research/research.md:299977-300002; /Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/999-hybrid-rag-fusion-upgrade/001-research-hybrid-rag-fusion-systems/005-mempalace/research/research.md:290683-290696]

**What it does**: All five reports accept ergonomic wrappers, aliases, helper surfaces, or protocol hints, but they repeatedly reject giving those additions their own storage, retrieval, or lifecycle authority.

**What it does**: Each external system makes at least one core tradeoff that Public should not inherit: Engram auto-creates sessions too freely, Mex over-centers markdown, Modus mixes review with read paths, Mnemosyne collapses identity and mutation too aggressively, and MemPalace makes forced hooks and palace/AAAK routing carry too much architectural weight.

**What it does**: Engram points toward passive capture and stable threads, Mex toward a provider-neutral doctor loop, Modus toward explicit review/due workflows, Mnemosyne toward governed convenience facades and plugin delegation, and MemPalace toward selective verbatim evidence plus a temporal fact sidecar.

**What it does**: Engram wants deterministic recent-session context, Mex wants a tiny maintenance and doctor loop, Modus wants compact doctor/debug output, Mnemosyne wants explicit compaction-time action guidance, and MemPalace wants tiny status/bootstrap hint blocks plus better observability and repair posture.

**What it does**: The continuity-oriented systems do not argue for a second memory engine; they argue for better timing. Engram optimizes the first resume glance, Mnemosyne optimizes compaction transport guidance, and MemPalace optimizes pre-compaction preservation plus bootstrap hints.

**What it does**: The strongest negative consensus is that Public should not regress from its hybrid, governed, session-aware architecture. Each system contributes useful ideas, but each system's core substrate is rejected as a replacement.

**Why it matters**: Public already has richer internals than any one external system, but the next safe wins are clearer "what to do now" surfaces over existing memory, bootstrap, and health primitives.

**Why it matters**: The cross-phase story is not "which system wins?" but "which boundary violations repeat?" The repeated answer is that whenever an external system fuses convenience with authority, the import becomes unsafe for Public's governance, provenance, and recovery model.

**Why it matters**: The disagreement is useful: it shows the unsolved product gaps are workflow-specific, not one big missing substrate. Public should choose the next feature by failure mode and operational value, not by conceptual novelty.

**Why it matters**: This gives Public a unified direction: improve the moments where context is most fragile, but do it with JSON-primary, fail-open, provenance-aware flows rather than interruptive or shadow-authority behavior.

**Why it matters**: This is the clearest cross-phase rule. Public should keep `memory_search`, `memory_context`, `session_bootstrap`, `generate-context.js`, CocoIndex, and code graph as the authorities, with any new surface compiling into them rather than competing with them.
**Why it matters**: This settles the import boundary: adopt patterns, not backends.

**What it does**: Late iterations explicitly identify the missing delivery contract: recall floors, latency budgets, token caps, storage ceilings, compaction-survival assertions, and ownership of PR vs nightly verification.
**What it does**: MemPalace separates mutable temporal facts from the verbatim corpus via a dedicated SQLite store with validity windows and invalidation semantics.
**What it does**: MemPalace shows the value of a pre-compaction preservation moment, while Public already has the safer pieces: a structured save authority and a compaction transport hook.
**What it does**: MemPalace’s `status` doubles as behavior instruction; Public already emits priming hints, structural routing nudges, and session-recovery guidance.
**What it does**: MemPalace’s L0/L1 wake-up stack proves the UX value of a compact startup read model, but Public already has the stronger recovery authority.
**What it does**: The late run repeatedly rejects the temptation to copy the external system’s storage posture, compression dialect, taxonomy rhetoric, or blocking-control-flow model.

**What it does**: The late-run synthesis converges on a stable rule: `memory_search`, `memory_context`, `session_bootstrap`, `generate-context.js`, CocoIndex, and code graph remain the authorities; any imported UX must compile into them rather than compete with them.
**What it does**: The phase packet still lacks the expected Level 3 closeout shape and reducer-owned state artifacts, and that absence was explicitly called out in the late iterations.

**What it does**: The repo’s own correction note and performance pass both undermine raw-default and AAAK-first postures, while the late synthesis points toward bounded evidence retention attached to structured memories instead.
**What it does**: The strongest portable pattern is better observability of writes and clearer repair posture, but not uncontrolled rebuilds, raw content logging, or extra repair authorities.
**Why it matters**: A formatter/profile may help startup continuity; a second bootstrap entrypoint would fragment the operator surface.
**Why it matters**: Research is complete enough to act on, but the packet is harder to validate, hand off, and reuse until the doc/state shape is normalized.
**Why it matters**: The importable value is not “add a new command,” but “teach the agent more directly at the existing bootstrap surfaces.”
**Why it matters**: This can improve operator trust quickly, but it can also create privacy, poisoning, and governance risk if it leaks beyond bounded evidence and `memory_health`-style control.
**Why it matters**: This closes the biggest design question of the 40-iteration run. The safe import strategy is now clear: patterns, not backends.
**Why it matters**: This is the only major idea that feels meaningfully net-new for Public without trying to replace existing retrieval or save authorities.

**Why it matters**: This is the strongest direct improvement for continuity and compaction survival, but only if it reuses Public’s existing save authority and never becomes a blocking shadow workflow.
**Why it matters**: This preserves the main advantage of verbatim evidence without importing MemPalace’s storage-growth and repair burden.
**Why it matters**: This prevents scope drift and protects Public’s governance, provenance, and continuity model.
**Why it matters**: Without measurable thresholds, the research stays advisory instead of executable.
Add bounded audit/repair evidence surfaces with redaction and kill switches.
Add bounded bootstrap/status protocol hints to existing surfaces.
Do not add a second storage/retrieval authority.
Do not copy periodic blocking hooks.
Do not make AAAK a primary startup/recall contract.
Do not make AAAK or palace taxonomy a required Public surface.
Do not replace `memory_search`, `memory_context`, `session_bootstrap`, `generate-context.js`, CocoIndex, or code graph.
Do not switch Public to raw-verbatim-by-default.
Do not turn palace taxonomy into a new routing authority.
Do not use periodic blocking hooks as the default preservation model.
Explicit rollout budgets and CI gates.
Fail-open compaction checkpointing using existing structured save authority.

Its strongest portable ideas are protocol guidance, compaction timing, and temporal fact separation. [SOURCE: external/mempalace/mcp_server.py:139-176; external/hooks/mempal_precompact_hook.sh:64-76; external/mempalace/knowledge_graph.py:56-150]
MemPalace’s own correction note narrows what is actually trustworthy in the external story. [SOURCE: external/README.md:52-79]
Normalize this research packet into canonical Level 3 closeout shape.
Preserve authority boundaries as an explicit architectural rule.
Prototype a token-capped wake-up/read profile as a formatter on `session_bootstrap`.
Prototype the temporal fact sidecar with invalidation, repair, and migration rules.

Public already has stronger core authorities and should keep them. [SOURCE: .opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/memory-search.ts:1-23; .opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/handlers/session-bootstrap.ts:163-209; .opencode/skill/system-spec-kit/scripts/dist/memory/generate-context.js:85-95]
Set rollout budgets: p95 startup/search latency, token caps, storage ceilings, compaction-survival assertions, CI ownership.
Ship compaction-time JSON-primary checkpointing in fail-open/advisory mode.

[approaches eliminated and why]
AAAK-first recall or startup compression as the main continuity mechanism.
Mandatory every-N-turn stop-hook blocking as the default save contract.
Raw-verbatim-by-default as the new Public storage posture.
Replacing `session_bootstrap` or `session_resume` with a separate palace-style wake-up authority.
Wing/hall/room taxonomy as the primary routing surface for Public memory or code retrieval.
Merge all new-feature candidates into one omnibus roadmap item, because the candidates optimize different failure modes and would obscure sequencing and ownership.
Pick one external system as the new north-star backend, because every phase keeps some ideas and rejects the system's authority model as a whole.
Treat cross-phase disagreement as unresolved research, because the disagreement is mostly about prioritization, not uncertainty about the safe architectural boundary.
Adopting AAAK as a primary startup or recall format.
